summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates Radhika Nagpal's Scientific American post about surviving tenure-track life at Harvard by rejecting the system's oppressive expectations. Nagpal's survival tactics include treating the job as a 7-year postdoc, ignoring advice, working fixed hours, and prioritizing being a whole person. O'Neil argues that people needlessly sacrifice their quality of life in high-stakes environments like academia and finance, believing misery leads to success. She contends this is cognitive bias and that the only way to avoid oppression is to live in denial of the system while still working hard toward personal goals.
key_points: Nagpal's key survival tactic was reframing tenure-track as a 7-year postdoc with no particular goal, freeing her from constant anxiety about measuring up
People in intense environments with slow weeding processes (academia, finance) become paralyzed by wondering if superiors think they're measuring up
The belief that misery and self-sacrifice lead to faster success is a cognitive bias, not reality
Ignoring oppressive systems while still working hard is both a survival tactic and a way to undermine those systems
This problem extends beyond academia to any high-stakes environment like D.E. Shaw's path to Managing Director
money_quotes: It's just amazing how often people are willing to make themselves miserable and delay their lives when they're going for something ambitious.
Why would anyone be more efficient at learning, at producing, or at creating when they're sleep-deprived and oppressed?
Ultimately it happens to someone when they start believing in the system.
The only way to avoid that kind of oppression is to live your life in denial of the system.
People spend months or even years in near-paralysis wondering if their superiors think they're measuring up. Gross!
Let's perfect the art of ignoring the system when it's oppressive, since it's a useful survival tactic, and also intrinsically changes the system in a positive way by undermining it.
key_insight: Rejecting oppressive systems by refusing to internalize their expectations is both a personal survival strategy and an act that undermines the system itself.
